The thing is, if we accept a replacement `input` variable in `get_loss()`, we should probably accept `target` there too.

When you create an `InputLayer`, you can also specify an input variable on construction and pass it to `get_output()`, so if we're going for symmetry we should support both of those here as well.

But my argument is that you only use `Objective` when you're using the internal input variable from `InputLayer` anyway (for symmetry reasons), so `Objective` does not need to support these 'overrides' (because you're not using them to begin with when you're using `Objective`).

To be honest I think we'd be better off if we rethought the entire API for this part of the library. It's just... not good :p

> I think this is an extreme example. Is there a use case where it would make sense to do something like this instead of creating the `InputLayer` with the right theano variable in the first place?

It's just another way of dealing with Lasagne networks, some people (including me) prefer to pass the Theano variable in the beginning, some prefer to pass it in the end, some prefer to use the one created automatically (which requires you to access the InputLayer in the end).

> One example could be a contractive-decontractive model with transposed convolution. Transposed convolution is a one-to-many operation. To select one of the many possible output shapes what I usually do is to save the symbolic shape of the each layer in the contractive pathway and pass it as a parameter to the constructor of the corresponding upsampling layer.

The intended solution would be to have the transposed convolution layer have two incoming layers and pick the input from one layer, the shape from the other, so it works even if you replace the input variable later.

> I would precompute expressions only when `get_output()` is called. The goal is to allow for multiple calls to `get_output`, but I don't see a reason to precompute something until it's being requested.

Okay, I was just checking. I thought you'd want some errors to be thrown earlier than now, on constructing the layer.

> I am not completely sure I am following, but I guess this refers to the case where you precompute everything.

Yes, if you propose to not compute anything automatically on layer construction, this won't be a problem.

> Setting the test_value on the input is easy, accessing it on the other layers is complicated. If you have a network with many layers and want to step through it and check the output of each layer to potentially intervene manually you have to call `get_output` on each of them.

That's an interesting use case, but it wouldn't require a change as drastic as implementing memoization. Note that `lasagne.layers.get_output()` accepts an input dictionary already that maps layers to Theano expressions. Any layers in this directory will be handled like input layers -- i.e., we will use the given expressions for their output and not traverse the layer graph past them. You can easily use this for memoization yourself:
```python
outputs = {}
layer = InputLayer()
layer = SomeLayer(layer)
outputs[layer] = lasagne.layers.get_output(layer, outputs)
layer = SomeOtherLayer(layer)
outputs[layer] = lasagne.layers.get_output(layer, outputs)
layer2 = SomeLayer(layer)
layer3 = SomeLayer(layer2)
layers = [layer2, layer3]
outputs.update(zip(layers, lasagne.layers.get_output(layers, outputs)))
```
To make this easier, we could also extend `get_output()` with an optional `cache` argument to be updated in place.
```python
cache = {}
lasagne.layers.get_output(layer, cache=cache)  # reuse any tensors already in the cache and update the cache with any new results
```
This would defer the ugly parts of memoization to the user (namely: where to memoize things and when to forget them), it would not complicate internals (`get_output` has such a cache dictionary anyway, it's just discarded after the call) and it would not change any existing behaviour.

> But just now it came to my mind that what you guys are constantly saying is that I could move my changes from lasagne.layers.DenseLayer to lasagne.layers.sparse.DenseLayer and do the same with the DropoutLayer, right?

Yes, `lasagne.layers.sparse` would house all layers (or adaptions of layers) that support sparse input and/or output via `theano.tensor.sparse`.
Note that you don't need to (and shouldn't) copy the full `DenseLayer` implementation over, but derive a new class from `lasagne.layers.dense.DenseLayer` and just override what you need (i.e., the `get_output_for()` method, since both the constructor and the output shape computation stay the same).
If we call it `lasagne.layers.sparse.DenseLayer`, then `lasagne.layers.sparse.*` will not be auto-imported into the `lasagne.layers` namespace (similarly to `lasagne.layers.dnn`), but needs to be imported separately. That's quite okay for a special-purpose module, though.

> > their behavior is also different (some configurations might be way, way slower with certain fallback implementations) and the numerical rounding behavior is different as well, so it would potentially lead to different results.

> Seriously?  This seems kind of crazy - shouldn't they all just compute convolutions?  And if not, isn't the idea that we created "functionally equivalent layers up to the extent possible"?  To whatever extent this is true, I think it needs to be heavily documented, as even as the code is I think people will assume the different layers will do the same thing.

The thing is that training a neural net is an iterative process, so small numerical differences start to matter a lot after a few 100 iterations. You can get completely different end results using different convolution implementations with the exact same initialization. In fact, due to the nondeterminism of the gradient in cuDNN, you can get completely different results with two subsequent runs (with the exact same initialization). So for reproducibility's sake, swapping implementations when not explicitly instructed to do so by the user is a bad idea.

> I guess what I'm not seeing here is how resampling (which will also have slightly different numerical results, but is fundamentally performing the same operation) is any different from convolution (a pretty precisely defined mathematical operation).  When you construct a convolutional layer, you're saying "convolve this please (with this # filters, kernel size, padding scheme, etc)" and you don't really care how it happens.  Maybe I am the one who needs the aforementioned documentation of how the different backends are different the most.

But the thing is, if you are using `Conv2DDNNLayer`, `Conv2DMMLayer` or `Conv2DCCLayer`, you *do* care how it happens - that's why you are using them in the first place. If you didn't care, you could just use `Conv2DLayer` and let it 'optimize' in the convolution backend of your choice. The alternative implementations exist precisely to give the user the option to go 'low-level' and manually select how things are implemented.

Currently, one exception to this is of course the fact that people resort to `Conv2DDNNLayer` because they want to use a feature that the default layer does not support (i.e. arbitrary padding). We should probably just implement this on `Conv2DLayer` as well so that doesn't occur anymore. Hopefully the new Theano convolution interface will already take care of this.

> > Most likely they would prefer to do what's necessary to make it available over using a different backend anyway.

> Not in my experience.  If someone doesn't have a GPU, they aren't going to buy a GPU box.

I wasn't thinking about buying hardware, but about installing missing dependencies (e.g. CuDNN, pylearn2). Honestly, if someone doesn't have a GPU and they want to train convnets, they should think twice about what they are doing :P But that is besides the point of course.

What I want to avoid is that people end up using a slow implementation while they think CuDNN is being used, because CuDNN was not installed properly. Things like that. I don't think warnings suffice. A good example of this problem is Theano itself: if you use `T.nnet.conv.conv2d`, it should use CuDNN if it is installed. If not, it will fall back to GpuCorrMM. It's happened to me more than once that I was 'accidentally' using GpuCorrMM because some sort of path wasn't set correctly. Quite annoying! (In fairness, Theano doesn't even generate a warning. But if there was a warning, I'm sure I would miss it the first few times I'm looking.)

> I would argue that we are not being transparent about the shortcomings, and that we already are doing "smart" things to make the layers as functionally equivalent as possible.

I think it's as transparent and 'dumb' as can be: `Conv2DLayer` wraps `T.nnet.conv.conv2d`, the others wrap specialized implementations. The problem is probably that there is too much of an incentive to use the specialized implementations right now. They were really intended to be used only by 'advanced users', so to speak. The new Theano convolution interface should change that at least.


---

Anyway, we are sort of mixing up two separate proposals:
1. unify the convolution interfaces so all implementations can be accessed using a single class
2. make this unified interface automatically 'fall back' if a chosen backend is not available

If #1 is done properly (i.e. not just throwing all of the code in a single class, which would be a completely unreadable monster), I could get behind that. I think #2 is a bad idea though.

One compromise for #2 could be that you could manually enable this 'fallback' behavior with another flag. It should be off by default though.

we also sent this to the wagtail mailing list (https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/wagtail/XYb6QQaiZk4), but it turns out this is really a wagtail-modeltranslation issue. here are my findings summarized:

this are the GET parameters sent by the ajax request upon clicking a "copy content" button:
- origin_field_name: "page_de"
- target_field_name: "page_content"
- serializedOriginField: [] (an empty list!)

Looking at the DOM, this is the button for copying the "DE" content:
<button class="translation-field-copy" current-lang-code="page_content" data-lang-code="page_de">de</button>

`copy_stream_fields.js` creates the buttons based on the stream fields existing in the DOM and uses simple plitting at "_" to determine the `fieldName` and `fieldLang`. In our model the streamfield is called `page_content`. so the "_" in the fieldname breaks the button creation code.

def arm_and_takeoff(aTargetAltitude):
    """
    Arms vehicle and fly to aTargetAltitude.
    """

    print "Basic pre-arm checks"
    # Don't let the user try to fly autopilot is booting
    if vehicle.mode.name == "INITIALISING":
        print "Waiting for vehicle to initialise"
        time.sleep(1)
    while vehicle.gps_0.fix_type < 2:
        print "Waiting for GPS...:", vehicle.gps_0.fix_type
        time.sleep(1)

    print "Arming motors"
    # Copter should arm in GUIDED mode
    vehicle.mode    = VehicleMode("GUIDED")
    vehicle.armed   = True
    vehicle.flush()

    while not vehicle.armed and not api.exit:
        print " Waiting for arming..."
        time.sleep(1)

    print "Taking off!"
    vehicle.commands.takeoff(aTargetAltitude) # Take off to target altitude

    while not api.exit:
        print " Altitude: ", vehicle.location.alt
        if vehicle.location.alt>=aTargetAltitude*0.95: #Just below
target, in case of undershoot.

            break;
        time.sleep(1)

@pgleeson there is a dedicated h.Random() instance which gets initialized using the Random123() method, which takes 3 seeds. The reason to use h.Random.Random123() is that it has advantages over Python's random methods. To ensure independence of streams and reproducibility the h.Random() instance is initialized as a function of 1) the feature its being used for, 2) the pre- and or post-syn cells involved, 3) a user-defined seed. All 3 are needed to avoid repetition of patterns during connection/stim creation. 

The reason it changed with respect to prev netpyne versions is that I had to modify the implementation to fix bugs or improve it. I'm hoping this version will be stable now. 

You can see where the h.Random is initialized with the 3 seeds by searching for "Random123" in network.py: https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/blob/development/netpyne/network.py

There are more details in these issues: 
https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/issues/89
https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/issues/185

Alright, let me know when there's any news. I would also suggest reconsider using binary PSKd representation internally, instead of JDC. For one, it makes more sense to work with an actual value rather than a representation of it, as less [core] code is needed to verify it's validity and it leaves a slightly lower program and RAM memory footprint. It is also simpler when PSKd is stored inside EEPROM or is displayed in other formats such as hex, QR or whatever is the most convenient for the manufacturer - that way at most only one decode operation is necessary.

@rlubos, you need to update the Windows user-mode implementation of the API [here](https://github.com/NordicSemiconductor/openthread/blob/465dac55ead18c46d4fc145dda813cf44cfc237d/examples/drivers/windows/otApi/otApi.cpp#L3501). That will entail the following:

1. Update the signature of the function for the new input.
2. Update the function itself to encode the variable in the buffer being sent to kernel mode.
3. Update the comment for `IOCTL_OTLWF_OT_COMMISIONER_ADD_JOINER` to indicate the new variable.
4. Decode the new variable when processing the `IOCTL` [here](https://github.com/NordicSemiconductor/openthread/blob/465dac55ead18c46d4fc145dda813cf44cfc237d/examples/drivers/windows/otLwf/iocontrol.c#L5581).

Hi @Derekkk,

You can actually use the softmax when performing multi-label classification (and this is the recommended method for now). At test time you can specify the number of label that should be predicted for each example, or the threshold above which labels should be predicted, using the following command:
```bash
./fasttext predict model.bin test.txt <K> <T>
```
This will predict *at most* `K` labels, which have a score larger than `T`. By default, `K=1` and `T=0.0`. If you want to predict *all* the labels which are above a given threshold, you should set `K` to the number of classes in your problem.

You can also try to train the model with sigmoid activation instead of the softmax, by using the negative sampling loss, with the command line option `-loss ns`. This will correspond to do one-vs-all training, where the negative labels are randomly sampled. However, this option is not supported at test time yet, since the softmax is still used to compute to label scores. We will probably implement full support for this in the near future.

Best,
Edouard

For stopping conditions it seems like there are two options:

1.  Use dask.delayed with a large graph of size `max_iter` and pass through a sentinel value if we should complete early.  We'll still run all of the tasks, but the latter part of them will run very quickly.

    This is nice because it uses only the local scheduler but introduces some overhead with very many tasks.

2.  Use dask.distributed and the concurrent.futures interface.  We would submit a few extra tasks to lead the computation, but would collect scores as they complete and not start any more computation if the stopping condition is met.

    This is nice because it is more efficient and dynamic, but introduces a requirement on the distributed scheduler

The crux of it is
- If you use C++11 string literals for your docstrings:
    - be careful to not indent your C++ code with actual tabulations because these will be part of the string and cause sphinx parsing to fail. Spaces are fine because sphinx trims the minimal indentation of the docstring.
   -  For normal Python docstrings, the first line is excluded from the minimal indentation computation so that you can write docstrings starting with `"""The beginning of the docstring`. In pybind11, since we also insert the signature, we must be careful to space-indent the one-line summary too.

I strongly support this, and I think this discussion has a huge overlap with #616. IMHO, valid values could be:

* `"from:a"`, `"from:b"`: same as now.
* `"prompt"`: when running `discover` the user is prompted for each collection, and this is saved with discovery data. Unlike the above, we can pick collections from either side, or skip collections (think filtering a `FULL OUTER JOIN`). Skipped ones are ignored.
* `"all"`: Create on each side the ones that don't exist in the other.
* `["named:somecol", "named:anothercol"]`: collections with a given *displayname*.

The big change, IMHO, is that `prompt` should show displaynames, rather than the collection names themselves.

An interesting alternative is a configuration wizard, which might save a lot of complexities for users (but move them to our side).

There's a slight benefit to doing it this way.

Previously, we tried to insert the directive, and if we found it at all, we told them and didn't bother trying to insert it and rolled everything back.

Now, we just stick it in (effectively) and try to start nginx. Because sometimes it's just a warning, not a failure.

So it's optimistically try to stick it in and start nginx anyway vs. go parse the file, test its contents for duplicates in the server block, and only if it all checks out stick it in.

Which gives us a chance of it succeeding vs. a better error message.

Another problem is that `analyze_step` is static (cannot not evaluate expressions without correct environment) so it can fail in complex cases (e.g. user-specified `output`).  So instead of trying to return all targets during analysis, it makes sense to return only matched output, and expand the DAG if more copies of the output is needed. So `execute()` is a bloking task to get data from the server. In the meanwhile we want an other (conrurrent) task to get the progress information and do something with it somewhere.
So i think we need a concurrent code.

```
def my_callback(num_rows, total_rows):
    # is called for each progress packet
    progress = <some code>
    print("here my progress: ", progress)

c = Client('localhost')
c.execute("SELECT ...", progress = my_callbak)
```
If `progress` is not `None` (and a callable) then it will be called for each new progress info.

A fun thing to deal with is that on Windows when a non-blocking connect fails, this is reported by marking the socket as "priority". Everyone else uses "readable" for this. Currently we hack around this the same way that some other libraries do, by making the windows `wait_socket_readable` function actually wait for either read or priority. If we wanted to support `wait_socket_priority` there as well then we'd need to figure out how let them coordinate.

I guess one option would be to remove the hack from the io layer, and instead have our `connect` method wait for both read and priority. With the current io API, this would require that `connect` open a nursery to call both `wait` functions simultaneously. That feels a little weird, but I guess would be ok? Are there workloads that care about getting maximum speed out of `connect` on Windows? Another option to consider would be redoing the API to allow a task to wait for several events at a time, like `wait_io_ready(flags)`. (Some connection here to https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/242, though that gets into much more complicated issues.)

Another Windows-related thing to consider is that if we switch to iocp-only (#52), then we won't have a way to wait for priority at all on that platform. Though fortunately we won't need to, either, since the iocp version of `connect` works differently.
